April 17, 2026

Busy Behind the Scenes: April 2026 Update from Sam's TNR

Thanks to generous donors, we now have funding for dozens of spay/neuter surgeries. Plus updates from our Quitman colony and heartwarming foster stories.

It's been a while since our last update, but that doesn't mean we've been idle. Behind the scenes, Sam's TNR has been making steady progress thanks to incredible community support, continued colony management, and some especially heartwarming foster work.

Thank You to Our Generous Donors

First and most importantly, we want to express our heartfelt gratitude to several very generous donors whose recent contributions have made a significant impact. Thanks to their support, we now have funding secured to spay and neuter several dozen cats based on our current veterinary partnerships and cost arrangements.

This means dozens of community cats won't contribute to the cycle of overpopulation, and hundreds of potential kittens will never be born to face uncertain futures. Your donations create real, immediate impact for cats in Brooks County.

Donor Impact

Several Dozen

Cats we can now spay/neuter with current funding

Hundreds

Future feral kittens prevented through your generosity

Quitman Colony Reaches Milestone: 20 Cats Altered

Our ongoing work at the Quitman colony continues to show steady progress. Just this week, we had two more cats from this location spayed, bringing the total number of cats in this colony to 20 spayed or neutered.

This milestone represents exactly why persistence matters in TNR work. What started as a few strays has become one of our most successful colony management sites. These 20 cats are now living healthier lives while contributing to a stable, managed population that doesn't produce new litters.

Quitman Colony Progress

2

Cats spayed this week

20

Total cats altered at this location

0

New litters expected from these 20 cats

Foster Stories: Giving Kittens a Chance

While our primary focus is TNR for population control, we're often asked to foster mother cats with kittens until the little ones are old enough for adoption. This work ensures that friendly cats and kittens don't face uncertain futures on the streets.

🐱 Mother with Six Kittens

Right now we're working with the Thomasville Humane Society, fostering a mother cat with six healthy kittens. Watching these babies grow stronger each day while their mother provides excellent care is one of the most rewarding aspects of our work.

Partnership spotlight: Working with established shelters like Thomasville Humane Society helps ensure these cats find loving, permanent homes through proper adoption channels.

🍼 Orphaned Kitten: Eyes Just Opening

We're also fostering an orphaned kitten whose eyes are just beginning to open! This tiny one requires around-the-clock care, but seeing those first glimpses of the world makes every 2 AM feeding worth it.

Critical care: Orphaned kittens this young require specialized feeding, warmth, and stimulation to survive and thrive.

💚 Forever Homes Ahead

The best part of foster work? Knowing these little ones will soon have forever homes. Each successful foster placement represents a life saved and a family enriched by the love of a rescued cat.

The Work Continues

Between colony management, foster care, and preparing for upcoming TNR efforts, Sam's TNR stays busy year-round. Every day brings new challenges and new opportunities to help community cats in Brooks County.

What makes this work sustainable is community support. From donors who fund surgeries to volunteers who provide foster care to neighbors who alert us to cats in need – it truly takes a community to create lasting change.

🤝 Community Impact

Inspired by our TNR efforts, a neighbor has joined in to help trap and spay a feral female cat. This kind of grassroots involvement is exactly how we create lasting change in our community. Thank you, Blaire!

We have also had caregivers of two larger feral cat colonies reach out to us for assistance. While we will soon have availability to help them, volunteers are always welcome to join our efforts in Brooks County to assist with trapping and transportation.

Help Us Keep Going

We currently have funding for several dozen spay/neuter surgeries, but the need in Brooks County is ongoing. Every donation directly funds the next cat's surgery and supports our foster efforts.

Your donation supports:

  • Spay/neuter surgeries for community cats
  • Foster care supplies for mothers and kittens
  • Transportation to veterinary appointments
  • Emergency medical care for injured cats

Sam's TNR is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit

All donations are tax-deductible and go directly to helping cats in need.
